title: TIE_FIGHTER vertices AND edges are the ROM's `.WP TIE` / `.WL TIE`
claim: 52 vertices at `.S=13.` (`.P -10,-16,18` -> [-130,-208,234] ... `.P -8,2,0` -> [-104,26,0]) and 90 undirected edges. Decoding `.WL TIE`'s AVG pen runs (.BD = blank-move then draw; .LD = continue the beam) yields exactly the port's edge set, including the seven inter-sub-body seam edges the polyline threads (source 1-7, 7-25, 25-37, 37-45, 45-31, 31-19, 19-13 -> model [0,6],[6,24],[24,36],[36,44],[44,30],[30,18],[18,12]).
reasoning: The `.P` macro is `.WORD .1'.*.S,...` — the trailing-dot suffix makes each operand DECIMAL times the object scale `.S=13.`, so vertices port unscaled and byte-for-byte. OBJDRW walks the `.WL` byte list as a flat polyline (point*4+code; code 0 = bright line, code 1 = blank move), matching the port's independent decoder in tests/core/tie-family-rom.test.ts. I re-verified that test's hand-transcription against WSOBJ.MAC:1352-1367 line-for-line and the whole suite is green (86/86). The lower cockpit cap is the ROM's five-edge pentagon (24-28) and the equator carries seven belt struts (the ROM omits 39-47) — both are the ROM's, not port artifacts.

title: The three exploded-TIE fragments (TI1/TI2/TI3) are the ROM's tables and draw lists
claim: TIE_WING_FRAG_1/2/3 carry the `.WP TI1` (18 pts), `.WP TI2` (18 pts, the same wing rotated (x,y,z)->(x,z,-y)) and TI3-cabin (28 pts, byte-identical to TIE_FIGHTER's aft slice) point tables at `.S=13.`, and their edges are `.WL TI1`/`.WL2 TI2` (one shared list) and `.WL TI3`. The wings' 6-12 fin<->strut stitch and TI3's 0-12/12-20/6-20 `.LD` beam-continuations — dropped by the old heuristic — are all present.
reasoning: TI2 vertices verified against WSOBJ.MAC:240-258 (`.P -10,18,2` -> [-130,234,26] ... `.P -3,0,-10` -> [-39,0,-130]). TI1/TI2 share draw list `.WL TI1`/`.WL2 TI2` (WSOBJ.MAC:1371-1372) so both decode identically; TI3's uncommented `.BD`/`.LD` lines (1389-1422) decode to the cabin edges. tests/core/tie-family-rom.test.ts pins all three set-equal to a hand oracle I re-checked against the source; suite green.

title: DARTH_TIE vertices AND edges are the ROM's `.WP RTH` / `.WL RTH`
claim: 56 vertices at `.S=10.` (`.P -18,-18,13` -> [-180,-180,130] ...) and exactly 72 undirected edges — the six pen-up sub-bodies of `.WL RTH` (two wing octagons, two strut squares, the body belts, and the +x front-window octagon). The ROM's one degenerate self-edge (`.BD 31,23,22,21,21,24,23` -> [20,20]) is correctly NOT copied.
reasoning: I re-checked tests/core/darth-tie-rom.test.ts's `.WL RTH` transcription against WSOBJ.MAC:1429-1482 (uncommented lines only) — faithful — and its decoder matches OBJDRW's polyline semantics. The suite is green: set-equal to the 72-edge oracle, 0/0 drift, self-edge dropped. This replaced story 8-10's heuristic that invented 44 edges and missed 12.

title: SURFACE_TOWER is the ROM's `.WP GND` table and `.WGD STB` stroke order
claim: 15 vertices = the shared `.WP GND` table minus its `.PGND 0,0,0` anchor, at `.S=30.*4`=120 with the height column read in HEX and recentred by GD$MDT=0xF00 (e.g. `.PGND -4,0,58` -> 0x58*120-3840 = 6720, not the decimal-reading 3120). The 13 edges are `.WGD STB`'s three profile polylines (BDRAWTO 1,3,15,12,9 / DRAWTO 7,10,13,1 / DRAWTO 2,14,11,8,7), 1-based minus one.
reasoning: I recomputed all 15 `.PGND` rows and they equal the port's vertices exactly; the height being hex (0x58=88, 0x52=82, 0x14=20) is confirmed by the `.PGND` macro's `.A'*.S` (no decimal suffix -> file radix 16). I hand-traced `.WGD STB` (WSOBJ.MAC:1769-1773) and its blank-then-draw runs reproduce the port's 13 edges. Column colour VGCYLW (WSGRND.MAC:841) matches render.ts TOWER_GLOW #ffd60a. tests/core/ground-objects-rom.test.ts green.

source.verbatim: .WGD STB		;STUB OF TOWER WITHOUT BUNKER HAT ON TOP

title: TOWER_CAP is the white (VGCWHT) section of `.WGD TWR`, a lossless colour split
claim: TOWER_CAP shares SURFACE_TOWER's `.WP GND` table and carries the 7 edges `.WGD TWR` strokes under M.GDCT (`DRAWTO 6,4,7` / `DRAWTO 5,4` / `BDRAWTO 7,9` / `BDRAWTO 7,8`): [8,5],[5,3],[3,6],[7,4],[4,3],[6,8],[6,7]. SURFACE_TOWER's 13 edges UNION this cap's 7 (2 shared) = exactly `.WGD TWR`'s 18 edges.
reasoning: Canvas strokes one colour per drawWireframe call, so the ROM's single `.WGD TWR` plot that switches pen mid-draw (M.GDCB yellow -> M.GDCT white) is split into two models. I hand-traced `.WGD TWR` (WSOBJ.MAC:1738-1754): the white runs are exactly TOWER_CAP's edges, and render.ts draws it CAP_GLOW #f4f4ff, matching WSGRND.MAC:861 'SO DRAW IT SPECIAL WHITE' (VGCWHT). Structure preserved, colour faithful.

title: SURFACE_BUNKER is `.WGD BNK` (base + near-bottom rings only), stroked VGCRED
claim: 8 edges from `.WGD BNK`'s BDRAWTO 1,2,14,13,1,3,15,13 / BDRAWTO 14,15 (1-based minus one): [0,1],[1,13],[13,12],[12,0],[0,2],[2,14],[14,12],[13,14]. It touches only the base (r=8) and near-bottom (h=6,r=6) rings — the 'SHORTY' truncated pyramid — never the column.
reasoning: Hand-traced `.WGD BNK` (WSOBJ.MAC:1719-1721) reproduces the port's 8 edges. Undamaged bunker colour is VGCRED (WSGRND.MAC:866 top/bunker colour) matching render.ts TURRET_GLOW red. tests/core/ground-objects-rom.test.ts green.

title: EXHAUST_PORT is the ROM's `.WP PORT` (three concentric squares, HEX) and `.WGD PORT`
claim: 12 vertices at `.S=8` read in HEX: `.PH 0C,0C,0` -> [96,96,0] (porthole, 0xC*8), `.PH 14,14,0` -> [160,160,0] (berm), `.PH 20,20,0` -> [256,256,0] (base). 18 edges = `.WGD PORT`'s three-pen plot (PLOT 5 / DRAWTO 9,8,4 / BDRAWTO 6,10,11,7 / DRAWTO 6,2 / BDRAWTO 6,4,0 / 4,5,1 / 5,7,3 / DRAWTO 2,0,1,3), 0-based.
reasoning: The `.PH` macro is `.1'*.S` (no decimal suffix -> file radix 16), so 0x20=32 not 20: the base row is 256, and a decimal reading (160) would collapse it onto the berm. I recomputed all 12 points and hand-traced `.WGD PORT` (WSOBJ.MAC:1857-1874) to the port's 18 edges. tests/core/exhaust-port-rom.test.ts green.

title: The enemy-fireball sparkle is the ROM's GNB0-3 / GNT0-3 gunshot pictures, exact
claim: render.ts FIREBALL_FRAMES are byte-for-byte the four `PSLABL GNB0..GNB3` spike deltas (each `AON 0,0` return-to-centre then `AON dx,dy` spike). GNB0 = (2,16),(15,8),(16,-6),(6,-10),(-6,-16),(-12,-8),(-16,2),(-6,8) — identical to the port. FIREBALL_FUSE_FRAMES are the four `GNT0..GNT3` fuse-ball spokes, also exact.
reasoning: The `AON` macro (WSVROM.MAC:117, `VCTR '.1'.*M./D.-CX,...`, comment 'ABSOLUTE DECIMAL COORDS') applies a decimal-suffix, so the operands are DECIMAL — the port reading '16' as sixteen is correct (a hex reading would non-uniformly rescale two-digit deltas and warp the shape). These pictures sit in WSVROM's inherited radix-16 region (before the :724 flip), but the macro's own decimal-ization governs. The ASPECT y-stretch (MASP/DASP=3/2, 'GIVES ROUND GUNSHOTS') is a CRT aspect correction the square-pixel canvas correctly omits; render re-scales to the projected body radius, so absolute magnitude is immaterial. Colour is VGCRED (WSVROM.MAC:487), matching FIREBALL_GLOW red.

title: The X-Wing and Y-Wing objects are correctly absent — they never assembled
claim: WSOBJ.MAC defines `.WP XW` / `.WP YW` point tables and `.WL XW` / `.WL YW` draw lists, but every one is wrapped in `.IF NE,0 ... .ENDC` (WSOBJ.MAC:366, 448, 1488, 1533) — a permanently-false guard, so no X-Wing or Y-Wing bytes exist in the ROM. The port's MODELS registry lists neither.
reasoning: A faithful clone must NOT ship rebel ships the cabinet never drew. `.IF NE,0` assembles its body only if 0!=0, which is never; both the vertex tables and the draw routines are inside such guards. models.ts's registry contains only the assembled objects. Trap avoided.

title: DEATH_STAR is a procedural sphere; the ROM draws an authentic 2D vector picture — and our comment claims none exists
claim: WSVROM has a full authentic Death Star: '.SBTTL DEATH STAR PICS ( DETAILED AND MINIATURE )' — BSHEM/BSCIR green hemisphere+circle (radius 50, `.S`=32 detailed / 1 miniature, VGCGRN), BSTRN the white equatorial-trench chord (VGCWHT, `AOFF 49,10 / AON -49,-9`), BSDSH+BSNSD the red superlaser dish (VGCRED, an offset ~17-radius circle), and BSFRM green 'farmland' surface hatching. Ours is a procedurally-generated 3D UV sphere (R=520, 8 stacks x 12 slices) whose doc comment asserts 'no authentic vertex table exists for it'.
reasoning: That provenance claim is refuted by the primary source: the pictures are VG-RAM-callable (`VJ BHEM`, `VJ BMIN`) and drawn in-game as flat billboards SCALED by the `M.` factor as the player closes, not as a rotating 3D body. The authentic look is a green disc + white trench line + red dish + green farmland — architecturally different from a lat/long sphere. This is the target of the entire game rendered on a wrong draw path. Fix is a draw-architecture change (a scaling 2D picture) plus porting the BSHEM/BSCIR/BSTRN/BSDSH/BSNSD/BSFRM point runs — hence size l. (BOOK_WAS_WRONG facet: the disassembly's silence, echoed in our comment, is contradicted by WSVROM.)

source.verbatim: .SBTTL	DEATH STAR PICS ( DETAILED AND MINIATURE )

refutation_corrections: Minor terminology: the authentic scale factor is the AVG-picture multiplier `M.=32.` (detailed) / `M.=1` (BMIN miniature), not `.S` (`.S` is the 3D `.WP`-object scale). Value 32 and its role as the picture scale are correct; the finding's `.S=32` label is imprecise, not wrong in magnitude. Also note the ROM object is literally a 2D vector PICTURE (no 3D vertex table), so our comment's phrase 'no vertex table' is narrowly true yet misleading, since authentic Death Star art is present.

title: TRENCH_TURRET is hand-authored; the authentic wall-gun table `.WP WGA` / `.WGD WGA` exists
claim: TRENCH_TURRET is a provisional 10-vertex box+barrel ([-30,0,-30]..[0,72,0]) whose comment says 'No authentic vertex table is directly portable'. WSOBJ.MAC ships `.WP WGA` (WALL GUN TYPE A, `.S=8`, 14 pts: a 4-corner wall base at +/-256/+/-192, a 6-pt gun body, a 4-pt nozzle) plus `.WGD WGA`/`.WGD2 WGB` drawn VGCRED. WGB shares the table (`.WPZ2 WGB`).
reasoning: The 'no portable table' claim is false — the disassembly lacked it but the primary MACRO-11 source has the full point table and draw routine. Our provisional geometry and scale bear no relation to WGA's. This is a data re-port (transcribe the 14 `.P` rows and decode `.WGD WGA`'s DRAWTO/BDRAWTO runs), so size m.

title: TRENCH_CATWALK is hand-authored; the authentic `.WP WFF` / `.WGD WFF` wall-force-field table exists
claim: TRENCH_CATWALK is a provisional 8-vertex horizontal girder. WSOBJ.MAC ships `.WP WFF` (WALL FORCE FIELD, `.S=8`, 6 pts: a 3-fin vertical structure rising from y=0 to y=512 at `.PH -20,0,0`..`.PH 0,40,20`) with `.WGD WFF` (DRAWTO 0,2,3,1,5,4,0) and its collided twin `.WGD WFG` (the flashing catwalk, VJFLS, `.WPZ2 WFG`).
reasoning: `.WGD WFG`'s comment 'CATWALK COLOR WHEN COLLIDED' confirms WFF/WFG IS the trench catwalk/force-field the port stands in for. The authentic shape (a tall 3-fin barrier) differs from our flat girder in both geometry and orientation. Data re-port of 6 `.PH` rows + the DRAWTO run; size m.

title: TRENCH is the ROM WALL PANEL `.WP WPN` plus 4 fabricated rails; TRENCH_SQUARE is a wrong stand-in for the same object
claim: TRENCH's 8 vertices are byte-identical to `.WP WPN` (WALL PANEL, `.S=8`: outer rect +/-256/+/-192, inner rect +/-128/+/-64), but `.WGD WPN` strokes ONLY the two rectangles (DRAWTO 1,2,3,0 / BDRAWTO 4,5,6,7,4) in VGCGRN — the port ADDS four 'catwalk rail' edges [0,4],[1,5],[2,6],[3,7] the ROM never draws. Separately, TRENCH_SQUARE (a hand-authored flat 80x80 square) is a second, geometrically-wrong attempt at the same WPN wall panel.
reasoning: There is exactly one such object in WSOBJ — WPN, the green wall panel — and both TRENCH ('Obj_Trench_Squares') and TRENCH_SQUARE derive from it. TRENCH's vertices are correct (CONFIRMED against `.WP WPN`); its 4 rails are fabrication (story 8-5 embellishment) that diverges from `.WGD WPN`. TRENCH_SQUARE ignores WPN's concentric-rectangle geometry entirely. Dropping the 4 rails to match `.WGD WPN` is size s; reconciling TRENCH_SQUARE onto WPN is part of the same re-port.

title: EXHAUST_PORT is stroked one amber colour; the ROM `.WGD PORT` uses three pens (green / turquoise / red porthole)
claim: `.WGD PORT` switches pen three times: `MOVD #VGCGRN...` (outer base), `MOVD #VGCTRQ...` (inner berm), `MOVD #VGCRED...!0FF ;PORTHOLE` (the +/-96 inner square). render.ts draws the whole plate a single PORT_GLOW amber (#ff9f0a) — a hue that is none of the ROM's three.
reasoning: Canvas strokes one colour per drawWireframe call, so a faithful three-pen render needs three passes (as SURFACE_TOWER/TOWER_CAP already do). The porthole in particular reads RED in the cabinet, not amber. models.ts flags this as a deliberate single-model simplification ('no AC asks for the colours'); recording it as an accepted divergence, low priority.

ours.verbatim: const PORT_GLOW = GLOW_FOR['Exhaust Port'] // exhaust-port target amber (shared)

title: The entire WSSTAR starfield subsystem is absent from the clone
claim: WSSTAR generates up to M$STNM=50 stars, bucketed into ST$EQQ=16 sectors ('DIVVY SCREEN INTO 4 BY 4'), seeded by RND8, moving relative to the player, culled and regenerated when they leave the view frustum (depth window 0x100..0xFFF). Two generators: VWSTAR (space, VGCWHT white) and VWSTRG (surface, VGCGRN green, 'GROUND HORIZON DROPPED', forced far out on the ground plane). The clone renders no starfield at all — no core state, no shell draw path.
reasoning: A moving 3D starfield is a core visual of the space and surface phases; its absence is a whole missing subsystem, not a wrong value. Porting it means a deterministic per-star generator in the pure core (seeded RNG, the 50-star pool, sector bucketing, the depth/FOV cull) plus a shell draw pass — size l.

title: WSOBJ explosion-piece objects (TW1-3, BK1-3, WG1-3) and WSVROM GNX0-3 have no ported model
claim: WSOBJ.MAC ships authentic explosion-shard objects — `.WP TW1/TW2/TW3` (tower-top pieces, `.S=3*4`), `.WP BK1/BK2/BK3` (bunker pieces), `.WP WG1/WG2/WG3` (wall-gun pieces, `.S=1`) — each with its own `.WL` draw list; WSVROM adds `LABL GNX0..GNX3` (gun-shot explosion sparkles). The clone has no corresponding models; its explosions are screen-space particle/ring effects.
reasoning: These are authentic per-object debris shards the cabinet flings when a tower/bunker/turret is destroyed. They are absent from models.ts. Whether to adopt them is an explosion-authenticity decision that belongs to the WSXPLD (pair-explosions) auditor; recorded here because the geometry TABLES live in my WSOBJ/WSVROM scope. The current FX approach is a defensible substitute, so wont_fix pending that team's call.
